_meta.note: Short historical-context sentences about each species' place in Toronto's planting history. Hand-written from the species posts + general urban-forestry knowledge.

acer platanoides: Toronto's #1 street tree by count. Planted heavily from the 1950s into the 1990s for its fast growth and tolerance of urban stress; no longer on the city's current planting list because it's invasive and outcompetes native maples in ravines.
gleditsia triacanthos: Ontario-native. Became the city's go-to replacement species after Dutch elm disease cleared the elms in the 1970s. Toronto plants mostly engineered thornless-seedless cultivars ('Skyline', 'Shademaster', 'Sunburst') — not the wild form.
tilia cordata: European littleleaf linden. A reliable shade-tree workhorse the city has planted steadily for decades. Fragrant yellow blooms in June are the main seasonal attraction.
picea pungens: Native to the Rocky Mountains, not Ontario. A mid-20th-century ornamental fad — lots planted 1960s–80s for the bluish needles. Rarely used for new plantings now.
quercus rubra: Native oak. Long-lived, good fall colour, increasingly favoured in current plantings for its ecological value — oaks support hundreds of native insect species. Many of Toronto's red oaks are post-2000 plantings and still relatively small.
gymnocladus dioicus: Kentucky coffeetree — Ontario-native, once endangered, now one of the city's favourite new-planting species. Tough, architectural, and distinctive.
acer freemanii: A 1982 silver × red maple hybrid engineered at the US National Arboretum for fast growth and brilliant red fall colour. The 'Autumn Blaze' cultivar is what you're seeing — everywhere, since the 2000s.
acer saccharinum: Native silver maple. Fast-growing, hugely popular through the mid-20th century; now planted sparingly because the wood is brittle and the roots lift sidewalks.
syringa reticulata: Japanese tree lilac — a compact, late-spring-blooming Asian import planted widely since the 1990s for smaller residential lots and under-wire positions.
ginkgo biloba: Living fossil from eastern China, sole survivor of a 270-million-year-old lineage. Toronto has 17,000+ on the streets, 88% of them less than 15 years old — a city-wide commitment to a tree with essentially no modern pests. Only male clones, to avoid the fruit.
malus sargentii: A compact Japanese flowering crabapple. Toronto has planted ~17,000 of them in residential contexts — small enough to fit where a full-size tree wouldn't.
celtis occidentalis: Hackberry — native, tough, underused. The city's planting program has leaned into it since the 2010s as an honest native alternative to the engineered cultivars.
acer saccharum: Sugar maple — the Canadian-flag species. Native, long-lived, slow-growing. Most big ones in Toronto are residential backyard survivors, not street-tree-program plantings.
amelanchier canadensis: Native serviceberry. White flowers in April, red berries for birds in June, orange-red fall colour. Popular since the 1990s for smaller front-yard boulevards.
pinus nigra: Austrian pine — European. Planted heavily 1960s–80s as a tough evergreen; now rarely used due to vulnerability to Diplodia tip blight.
pyrus calleryana: Callery pear, Asian origin. The 'Chanticleer' cultivar was a 1980s-90s favourite for its tight crown and spring bloom. Falling out of favour because the species has turned invasive in parts of the US.
ulmus pumila: Siberian elm — Asian. Planted in the mid-20th century as a Dutch-elm-disease-resistant stand-in. Disappointing in practice; no longer recommended.
picea glauca: White spruce — native to the boreal forest. Long-lived but slow. Mostly older plantings.
tilia americana: American basswood — native. Much less planted than its European cousin but ecologically superior.
platanus acerifolia: London plane — a hybrid of American sycamore × Oriental plane. Famous for mottled bark, extreme urban tolerance, and size. Some of Toronto's biggest street trees are London planes.
ulmus americana: American elm — was the defining tree of Toronto's streets a century ago. Dutch elm disease killed most of them in the 1960s-70s. Current survivors are kept alive with periodic fungicide injections; the 'Valley Forge' cultivar is newly-planted for disease resistance.
betula papyrifera: Paper birch — native to northern Ontario forests, planted as an ornamental in Toronto for the white bark. Struggles in heat; increasingly rare in new plantings.
quercus macrocarpa: Bur oak — the signature species of Ontario's lost oak savannas. Native, long-lived (300+ years). Some of Toronto's veteran bur oaks predate the city itself.
acer rubrum: Red maple — native, fast-growing, brilliant red fall colour. A contemporary planting favourite alongside Freeman maple.
liriodendron tulipifera: Tulip tree — Carolinian, native to the southernmost tip of Ontario. Planted as an architectural specimen tree for its tall straight growth habit and orange-green flowers.
catalpa speciosa: Northern catalpa — native to the American Midwest. Huge heart-shaped leaves, white-panicle blooms in June, long bean-like pods. Planted more for specimen character than systematic programs.
robinia pseudoacacia: Black locust — native to the Appalachian range, at the edge of its natural range here. Honey-scented white flowers in early June, extraordinarily rot-resistant wood. Planted sparingly; the 'Purple Robe' cultivar has pink flowers.
cercidiphyllum japonicum: Japanese katsura — Asian origin. Heart-shaped leaves with apricot-peach fall colour that smells of caramel or burnt sugar when fallen. A prized specimen tree in recent plantings.
fagus sylvatica: European beech. Smooth grey bark, leaves held into winter as papery bronze. The city plants it sparingly; most big ones in Toronto are private-property heritage trees.
aesculus hippocastanum: Common horsechestnut — European. Large white-panicle flowers in May, conkers in fall. An older specimen-tree choice.
cercis canadensis: Eastern redbud — Carolinian, at the northern edge of its native range in Toronto. Magenta-pink blooms on bare branches in late April before anything else is out. Planted in small-lot contexts since the 1990s.
